provenance.source_corpus: Failure of natural regeneration after clearcut logging in Wood Buffalo National Park, Canada

source_spans.text: Logging of boreal riparian old-growth white spruce took place in Wood Buffalo National Park from 1951 to 1991.
Failure of post-logging natural regeneration in Wood Buffalo National Park is attributable primarily to two factors
Clearcutting and site preparation are shown to degrade boreal riparian ecosystem structure and function.

In the cutblocks of TB408, there are virtually no snags (median 0 ha-1, 0 m tall, 0 cm dbh, n = 21 sites); in undisturbed spruce and spruce-balsam poplar forests, median values are 67 ha-1, 3.5 m tall, 21.0 cm diameter, n = 17 sites

nodes.name: Distance to nearest white spruce seed tree

source_spans.text: White spruce seed tree distance is important in determining the ability of a clearcut to regenerate naturally.
Clearcutting and site preparation are shown to degrade boreal riparian ecosystem structure and function.
Of all site preparation techniques, windrowing and blade scarification cause the most soil degradation in British Columbia

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:030291f4eb20
edges.original_sentence: Failure of post-logging natural regeneration in Wood Buffalo National Park is attributable primarily to two factors: (1) large size of the clearcuts, placing most of any area logged beyond the effective dispersal distance of white spruce seed; (2) destruction of advance growth and lack of residual growth.
